I am on a Mac computer working on adding anchors. Why does it underline my working?

Ducky (AI tutor)

Hi Barbara! It sounds like you're working on adding anchors in your HTML, and you're noticing that your text is being underlined. This is a common behavior, especially when you're using anchor tags (<a>).

Why Are Anchors Underlined?

By default, browsers style anchor tags with an underline to indicate that they are clickable links. This is a standard convention in web design to help users recognize links easily.

How to Change the Underline Style

If you want to remove the underline from your anchor tags, you can use CSS (Cascading Style Sheets). Here’s a simple example:

html
html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Anchor Example</title>
    <style>
        a {
            text-decoration: none; /* This removes the underline */
            color: blue; /* You can change the color as well */
        }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <a href="#section1">Go to Section 1</a>
    <div id="section1" style="margin-top: 1000px;">This is Section 1</div>
</body>
</html>

Questions to Consider

  • Are you trying to keep the underline for some links but remove it for others?
  • Would you like to style your links differently, such as changing their color or adding hover effects?

Feel free to share your code or any specific styles you're trying to achieve, and I can help you further!

We have not done CSS or talked about “Flex box”.

Ducky (AI tutor)

It sounds like you're eager to learn about CSS and Flexbox, which are essential tools for web design! Since you mentioned that you haven't covered these topics yet, let’s start with the basics.

What is CSS?

CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is a language used to style and layout web pages. It allows you to control the look and feel of your HTML elements, such as colors, fonts, spacing, and positioning.

What is Flexbox?

Flexbox, or the Flexible Box Layout, is a CSS layout model that makes it easier to design complex layouts and align items within a container. It allows you to position items dynamically, which is super useful for responsive design.

Key Concepts of Flexbox

  1. Container and Items: To use Flexbox, you need a container element that will hold the items you want to layout.
  2. Display Property: You set the container to display: flex;. This changes the way the child elements (the items) are laid out.
  3. Flex Direction: You can control the direction of the items using the flex-direction property. For example, row (default) places items side by side, while column stacks them vertically.

Example Code

Here's a simple example to illustrate how Flexbox works:

html
html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Flexbox Example</title>
    <style>
        .container {
            display: flex; /* Enables Flexbox */
            justify-content: space-between; /* Distributes items evenly */
        }
        .item {
            background-color: lightblue;
            padding: 20px;
            margin: 10px;
            flex: 1; /* Items will grow equally */
        }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <div class="container">
        <div class="item">Item 1</div>
        <div class="item">Item 2</div>
        <div class="item">Item 3</div>
    </div>
</body>
</html>

In this example:

  • The .container class applies Flexbox to hold three items side by side.
  • The justify-content: space-between; property spaces the items evenly across the container.

Follow-Up Questions

  • Have you started experimenting with CSS in your projects yet?
  • Would you like to dive deeper into specific Flexbox properties, like align-items or flex-wrap?
  • Is there a particular layout or effect you’re trying to achieve with Flexbox?

Feel free to share your current code or any specific challenges, and we can work through them together! Remember, understanding Flexbox will make your layouts much easier to manage, especially as you progress in your web development skills.

It is not letting me copy by code form the box

Ducky (AI tutor)

It sounds like you're having trouble copying code from a specific box. Here are a few things you can try:

  1. Click and Drag: Make sure you're clicking and dragging your mouse to highlight the code. Sometimes, if you don't start from the very beginning, it might not select everything.

  2. Right-Click Menu: After highlighting the code, try right-clicking and selecting "Copy" from the context menu.

  3. Keyboard Shortcuts: You can also use keyboard shortcuts:

    • On a Mac, press Command + C to copy after highlighting the code.
    • On Windows, press Ctrl + C.

  4. Check Browser Settings: Sometimes, browser settings or extensions can interfere with copying. Try using a different browser or disabling any extensions temporarily.

  5. Screenshot: If all else fails, you could take a screenshot of the code and refer to it that way.

If you're still having trouble, could you let me know what platform or tool you're using to view the code? That might help me provide more specific advice!

Here is my code

Ducky (AI tutor)

It looks like you're sharing a code snippet with some anchor tags and a paragraph. Let's take a closer look at the issues you've mentioned and how we can improve your code!

HTML Code Snippet

Here's a basic example of what your HTML might look like based on your description:

html
html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Links Example</title>
    <style>
        a {
            text-decoration: none; /* Removes underline */
            color: blue; /* Sets link color */
        }
        a:hover {
            text-decoration: underline; /* Underline on hover */
        }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <p>
        <a href="https://nprsa.kpb.us">My Favorite Family Fun All Year Long just outside Kenai, Alaska on the North Road.</a>
    </p>
    <p>
        <a href="https://www.amazon.com">Search on Amzson.</a> <!-- Typo here -->
    </p>
    <p>
        <a href="https://www.apple.com/store">The Apple Store for all your needs.</a>
    </p>
</body>
</html>

Issues Identified

  1. Typo in the Second Link:

    • The displayed text says "Amzson" instead of "Amazon." You can correct it like this: 
      html
      html
      <a href="https://www.amazon.com">Search on Amazon.</a>

  2. Styling the Links:

    • To make your links more visually appealing, you can add some CSS. In the example above, I've included a simple style that removes the underline by default and adds it back on hover.

Questions for You

  • Are you looking to add more styles to your links or the overall page?
  • Do you have any specific functionality in mind for these links, like opening in a new tab?
  • Would you like to explore more about CSS to enhance your web page design?

Feel free to share your code or any specific challenges you're facing, and we can work through them together! If you have a screenshot of your code, you can share it using the paperclip icon for more specific help.

alt="Ford">

Ducky (AI tutor)

Sure! It sounds like you need help with the <img> tag in HTML. The <img> tag is used to embed images in a web page. Here’s a basic overview of how to use it:

Basic Syntax

html
html
<img src="URL_of_the_image" alt="Description of the image" width="300" height="200">

Attributes Explained

  • src: This is the source attribute that specifies the path to the image you want to display. It can be a relative path (if the image is in your project folder) or an absolute URL (if the image is hosted online).
  • alt: This attribute provides alternative text for the image if it cannot be displayed. It’s also important for accessibility, as screen readers will read this text to users who cannot see the image.
  • width and height: These attributes specify the dimensions of the image in pixels. You can also use CSS for styling instead.

Example

Here’s an example of how to use the <img> tag:

html
html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Image Example</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>My Favorite Image</h1>
    <img src="https://example.com/my-image.jpg" alt="A beautiful scenery" width="600" height="400">
</body>
</html>

Questions for You

  • What specific issues are you facing with the image tag?
  • Are you trying to display an image from a URL or from your local files?
  • Do you need help with styling the image or making it responsive?

Feel free to share your code or any specific challenges you're encountering, and I can help you further!

Ducky (AI tutor)

It looks like you're trying to display an image using an <img> tag in HTML. The code you provided is a valid way to embed a base64-encoded image directly into your HTML. Here's a breakdown of the code:

html
html

alt="Ford">

Explanation:

  • <img> Tag: This is the HTML tag used to embed images.
  • src Attribute: This specifies the source of the image. In this case, it's a base64-encoded PNG image.
  • alt Attribute: This provides alternative text for the image, which is useful for accessibility and in case the image fails to load.

Questions to Consider:

  • Are you trying to display this image on a webpage? If so, make sure your HTML is properly structured.
  • Do you want to style the image (e.g., size, borders)? You can use CSS for that.
  • Is there a specific issue you're facing with this image tag?

Feel free to share more details or ask any specific questions you have!

show the picture

Ducky (AI tutor)

It looks like you're trying to display an image using an <img> tag in HTML. If you have a specific image you'd like to show, you can use the following format:

html
html
<img src="URL_of_the_image" alt="Description of the image">

Example

If you have a base64-encoded image or a URL, you can replace URL_of_the_image with that. For example:

html
html
<img src="https://example.com/my-image.jpg" alt="A beautiful scenery">

Questions to Consider

  • Are you trying to display this image on a webpage? If so, make sure your HTML is properly structured.
  • Do you want to style the image (e.g., size, borders)? You can use CSS for that.
  • Is there a specific issue you're facing with this image tag?

Feel free to share more details or ask any specific questions you have!

what is wrong with this one of ford. alt="Ford">

Ducky (AI tutor)

It looks like you're trying to display a base64-encoded image of a Ford using the <img> tag in HTML. However, there seems to be a small issue with the src attribute in your code. The src attribute should not start with https:// when you're using a base64-encoded image. Instead, it should start with data:image/png;base64,.

Here's the corrected version of your code:

html
html
<img alt="Ford">

Key Points:

  • Base64 Format: Ensure that the src starts with data:image/png;base64, without the https:// prefix.
  • Image Display: If the image still doesn't display, check if the base64 string is complete and valid. You can test it in an online base64 image decoder to see if it renders correctly.

Questions to Consider:

  • Are you seeing any error messages in the browser console?
  • Have you tried testing the base64 string in a different environment or tool to confirm it's valid?
  • Would you like to know how to convert images to base64 format if needed?

Feel free to share more details or ask any specific questions you have!

👀

✅

🙌

Omair Khoder

03:21

so generally speaking you're almost there, what you need to do is to find a specific width where it would be best to hide the nav bar items, and show the burger menu, and use a media query with that size to do that


so let's remove your current media queries and add this one instead:


@media (max-width: 768px) {
  .nav__link--list {
    display: none;
  }
  .btn__menu {
    display: block;
  }
  .header__description {
    flex-direction: column;
    text-align: center;
  }
  .header__img--wrapper {
    width: 90%;
  }
}


with this when the screen has the width of 768px the nav links would disappear, and the menu button would show and it would be functional


remove all other media queries where you're setting the sizes for these

👀

✅

🙌

Barbara Ralston

03:23

I will try it. Thank you

👀

✅

🙌

Omair Khoder

03:23

for sure you're most welcome!

👀

✅

🙌

03:24

this would fix the design for the navbar on both screens mobile and desktop, and would fix the responsiveness of the header on larger screens as well

👀

✅

🙌

03:24

of course you still need to work a bit on the sizes for the header, but currently you need only one media query and it's the one I sent, you can add the remaining styles for all remaining sections inside of it,

Project 8: React

Saturday, 28 Mar 202610:00 AM - 11:00 AM

In this final capstone project, you’ll build a complete React application from start to finish. You’ll combine components, state management, hooks, and performance best practices into a fully functional, production-ready app. Part of 2026 Q1 series.

Project 7: JavaScript

Thursday, 26 Mar 20268:00 AM - 9:00 AM

Wednesday, 25 Mar 20269:00 AM - 10:00 AM

JS 6: Async JavaScript

Thursday, 05 Mar 20269:00 AM - 10:00 AM

In this session, you’ll learn how JavaScript handles tasks that take time, like loading data from the web. We’ll cover promises and fetch, then move into async/await so you can write asynchronous code that’s clear and reliable. Part of 2026 Q1 series.

React 5: Advanced Markup

Mindset Workshop 1

Friday, 19 Dec 202510:00 AM - 11:00 AM

Join our upcoming Mindset Workshop, part of our live session series, where you’ll gain practical techniques to strengthen your mindset, boost self-confidence, and cultivate lasting resilience. Facilitated by Blane, this session is designed to help you shift perspective, break through mental barriers, and move forward with clarity and purpose.

Zoom Video

Dev Workshop 2 — Git & GitHub Fundamentals

Thursday, 11 Dec 202511:00 AM - 12:00 PM

You’ll learn how developers save, track, and share their code using Git and GitHub. This session shows you how to avoid breaking your work and collaborate confidently with others. Time and date are subject to change.

Zoom Video

Advanced 3: React Advanced

Saturday, 22 Nov 20254:00 PM - 5:00 PM

Take your React skills to the next level! In this lecture, you’ll explore how React manages complex data and behavior through hooks, context, and routing. You’ll also learn how to handle component lifecycles for efficient, predictable, and scalable React applications.

Zoom Video

React

Advanced 2: React Basics

Friday, 21 Nov 20255:00 AM - 6:00 AM

Learn how modern web apps are built with React — a powerful library for building interactive user interfaces. In this lecture, you’ll understand how to structure a React project, create reusable components, pass data with props, and manage dynamic behavior with state. Passcode:137366.

Zoom Video

React

Advanced 1: Tailwind CSS

Tuesday, 18 Nov 20253:00 PM - 4:00 PM

Discover how to design fast, modern, and responsive websites using Tailwind CSS — a utility-first framework that lets you style directly in your HTML. This lecture will guide you through setup, utility classes, layouts, and responsive design principles to build clean and efficient interfaces quickly.

Zoom Video

React

JS 4: DOM, Events & APIs

Friday, 14 Nov 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

Discover how JavaScript brings web pages to life! In this lecture, you’ll learn how to dynamically manipulate the DOM, respond to user actions with events, and connect your apps to external data through APIs and local storage - the foundation of interactive web applications.

Zoom Video

CSS 4: Responsive Design & Flex/Grid

Wednesday, 12 Nov 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

Learn how to make your websites look great on every screen. This lecture explores responsive web design techniques, flexible layouts using Flexbox and Grid, and best practices for optimization and accessibility to ensure your sites are both adaptive and user-friendly.

HTML/CSS

CSS 3: Advanced CSS & Effects

Wednesday, 12 Nov 20256:00 AM - 7:00 AM

Project Lesson (HTML) – Semantic HTML

Tuesday, 07 Oct 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

Come build a tiny, polished site with semantic HTML. We’ll add images (URL & local), Font Awesome icons, link a stylesheet, and do some debugging. Practice makes perfect! All files included - open and go!

Files

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

💡 Practice Lesson (JS) - Arrays & Objects

Friday, 03 Oct 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1️⃣Select and target HTML elements using JavaScript (getElementById, querySelector). 2️⃣Attach event listeners to buttons for interactivity (addEventListener). 3️⃣Toggle a modal open/close state using JavaScript. 4️⃣Debug common DOM issues (wrong selectors, missing event listeners, CSS class not applied). 5️⃣Have answers to the most common DOM/JS beginner questions.

Zoom Video

Javascript

💡 Practice Lesson (CSS) - Flexbox

Wednesday, 01 Oct 202511:00 AM - 12:00 PM

By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1️⃣ Understand how to apply core Flexbox properties (display: flex, justify-content, align-items, flex-direction) to create layouts. 2️⃣ Build a header or section using Flexbox. 3️⃣ Debug common CSS alignment issues (e.g., misaligned modal, items not centering). 4️⃣ Have answers to the most common questions.

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

💡 Practice Lesson (HTML) - Semantic HTML

Monday, 29 Sep 202512:00 PM - 1:00 PM

By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1️⃣ Understand how to structure an HTML page using semantic tags instead of “div soup.” 2️⃣ Create a component using HTML. 3️⃣ Debug broken HTML structures (e.g., unclosed tags, incorrect nesting). 4️⃣ Get answers to the most common questions.

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

🧠 Concept Classes (JS) - Arrays & Objects

Saturday, 27 Sep 20258:00 PM - 9:00 PM

By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1️⃣ Understand what arrays and objects are — and when to use each 2️⃣ Create and access array values by index 3️⃣ Add/remove items using common array methods (push, pop, shift, unshift, slice, splice) 4️⃣ Understand how object key-value pairs work and how to access or update them 5️⃣ Choose whether a structure needs an array, an object, or both 6️⃣ Read small code snippets and predict their output

Zoom Video

Javascript

🧠 Concept Classes (CSS) - Flexbox

Thursday, 25 Sep 20255:00 PM - 6:00 PM

By the end of this session, students will be able to: Understand what Flexbox is and when to use it Set up a Flexbox layout using display: flex Use core Flexbox properties: flex-direction, justify-content, align-items flex-wrap, flex-flow, order, align-self, align-content Debug common Flexbox layout issues with DevTools Build and style real-world UI components using Flexbox

Slides

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

🧠 Concept Classes (HTML) - Semantic HTML

Tuesday, 23 Sep 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1️⃣ Understand what semantic HTML means and why it matters 2️⃣ Identify key semantic tags used in modern page layouts 3️⃣ Use <header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, <article>, <footer> appropriately 4️⃣ Build a clean, semantically structured HTML layout 5️⃣ Visually connect structure with purpose (accessibility, SEO, maintainability)

Slides

Friday, 12 Sep 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1️⃣ Build a consistent and realistic coding practice routine 2️⃣ Remix existing projects to improve comprehension and creativity 3️⃣ Use interactive tools to target skill gaps in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript 4️⃣ Access resources and platforms to become confident self-learners

🧪 Dev Workshop - Git & GitHub 101

🧪 Dev Workshop - Google Smarter, Not Harder

Macro Calculator - HTML (Live Session)

Monday, 02 Jun 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

In this session, we’ll go over the HTML structure of the Macro Calculator project. We'll break down how the form was set up to collect user input like age, weight, height, and activity level, and how semantic tags were used to organize the layout. You'll get a clear picture of how the foundation for user interaction was built.

Files
Slides

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

Week 2 - React (Live Session)

Saturday, 31 May 20255:00 PM - 6:00 PM

In this session, we’ll walk through key parts of the React project, focusing on how components, props, state, and other core concepts are used to build dynamic and interactive interfaces. We’ll break down how the code is structured, how different pieces work together, and how React makes the development process more efficient. The goal is to deepen your understanding of how React is applied in real projects.

Zoom Video

React

Digital Vision Board - JavaScript Part 2 (Live Session)

Friday, 30 May 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

We’ll finish the walkthrough by explaining how local storage was used to persist user data. You’ll see how JavaScript logic is used to save and retrieve goals, images, and notes so the board keeps its content between sessions. We'll also cover basic structures and logic used to manage this data.

Files
Slides

Zoom Video

Javascript

Digital Vision Board - JavaScript Part 1 (Live Session)

Thursday, 29 May 20255:00 PM - 6:00 PM

In this session, we’ll review how JavaScript was used to make the board interactive. We’ll explain how form submissions were handled, how the DOM was updated with user input, and how different elements are dynamically displayed. This is all about understanding how interactivity was introduced.

Zoom Video

Javascript

Digital Vision Board - CSS Part 2 (Live Session)

Wednesday, 28 May 202511:00 AM - 12:00 PM

We’ll continue the walkthrough by reviewing advanced styling features added to the board. This includes hover effects, transitions, and styling for elements like cards and buttons. We'll also explain how the board's responsiveness and visual hierarchy were improved through CSS.

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

Digital Vision Board - CSS Part 1 (Live Session)

Tuesday, 27 May 202511:00 AM - 12:00 PM

This session covers the first stage of styling the Digital Vision Board. We’ll go over how Flexbox was used for layout, how color schemes and typography were chosen, and how spacing was handled. You'll get a breakdown of the core styling decisions that shape the user interface.

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

Digital Vision Board - HTML (Live Session)

Monday, 26 May 20259:00 AM - 10:00 AM

In this session, we’ll walk through the HTML structure of the Digital Vision Board project. We’ll explain how the layout is organized using semantic tags and how form elements are used to capture goals, image URLs, and motivational notes. The focus will be on understanding how the foundation of the project was set up.

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

Week 1 - React (Live Session)

Saturday, 24 May 20255:00 PM - 6:00 PM

In this session, we’ll kick off the React session with a high-level overview of what React is, why it’s used, and how it fits into modern front-end development. React helps build dynamic, interactive user interfaces. This session is designed to set the foundation for everything you'll be doing with React in the weeks ahead.

This week, we will be setting up a Tasks dashboard project using frontend development tools. In this session, we will be going over the Javascript (part 2) for that project.

TasksSimplified - HTML (Live Session)

HTML/CSS

Week 10 - HTML & CSS Part 2 (Live Session)

Saturday, 26 Apr 20257:00 AM - 8:00 AM

Live sessions designed to provide interactive learning experiences for students across key modules: HTML & CSS, JavaScript, and React. This session will be led by a dedicated mentor who will guide students through hands-on problem-solving, code walkthroughs, and Q&A sessions.

Zoom Video

HTML/CSS

Week 10 - Javascript Part 2 (Live Session)

Thursday, 24 Apr 20256:00 PM - 7:00 PM

In this interactive and final session of the block, we covered essential web tools and modern coding techniques based on popular student requests. The instructor walked through updated methods for implementing Google Fonts and Font Awesome, addressing recent UI and API changes that have caused confusion. We also explored the Fetch API with real-world examples—highlighting how to handle asynchronous data fetching, errors, and best practices in modern JavaScript. The session wrapped up with a Q&A and a combined example tying everything together.

Zoom Video

Javascript

Week 10 - Javascript Part 1 (Live Session)

Wednesday, 23 Apr 20258:00 AM - 9:00 AM

Victor Alves' session focused on building the Module 4 final project, specifically implementing the JavaScript functionality for a movie search app. He walked through capturing user input with onchange, accessing the value with event.target.value, and fetching data from the OMDb API using async/await. The goal is to dynamically search and display movies based on user input, with more functionality to be completed in the next session.

Zoom Video

Javascript
